facts.text: The word 'photography' comes from Greek words meaning 'drawing with light.'
The first permanent photograph was taken by Joseph Nicéphore Niépce in 1826, requiring an exposure time of about 8 hours.
Ansel Adams developed the Zone System, a technique for controlling exposure and development in black-and-white photography.
The exposure triangle consists of three elements: aperture, shutter speed, and ISO, which together control image brightness.
Canon introduced the EOS system in 1987, which became one of the most popular camera systems in history.
A prime lens has a fixed focal length, while a zoom lens can vary its focal length across a range.
Steve McCurry's 'Afghan Girl' appeared on the cover of National Geographic in June 1985 and became one of the most recognized photographs ever.
Golden hour, the period shortly after sunrise or before sunset, produces warm, soft light prized by photographers.
DJI controls over 70% of the global consumer drone market, making aerial photography accessible to millions.
The daguerreotype, invented by Louis Daguerre in 1839, was the first commercially practical photographic process.
Henri Cartier-Bresson is often called the father of modern photojournalism and coined the concept of 'the decisive moment.'
Bokeh refers to the aesthetic quality of the out-of-focus areas in a photograph, often produced by wide apertures.
The Pulitzer Prize for Feature Photography has been awarded annually since 1968 to recognize outstanding photojournalism.
Adobe Photoshop was first released on February 19, 1990, and revolutionized digital image editing.
Dorothea Lange's 'Migrant Mother' (1936) became an iconic image of the Great Depression and is one of the most reproduced photos in history.
The rule of thirds divides an image into nine equal parts using two horizontal and two vertical lines, placing subjects at intersections for stronger composition.
Leica introduced the first commercially successful 35mm camera, the Leica I, in 1925, popularizing the 35mm format.
Long exposure photography uses slow shutter speeds to capture motion blur, creating effects like silky waterfalls or light trails.
GoPro was founded in 2002 by Nick Woodman, who built the first prototype from a 35mm camera and a wrist strap while surfing in Australia.
The iPhone's first camera in 2007 was only 2 megapixels and couldn't even record video.
Hasselblad cameras were used by NASA astronauts during the Apollo missions to photograph the Moon and Earth from space.
Aperture is measured in f-stops; a lower f-number means a wider opening and shallower depth of field.
The World Press Photo contest has been held annually since 1955 and is one of the most prestigious photojournalism awards.
Richard Avedon revolutionized fashion photography by bringing movement and emotion to his subjects, departing from static poses.
A gimbal stabilizer uses motors and sensors to counteract camera shake, enabling smooth handheld video footage.
Kodak engineer Steven Sasson invented the first digital camera in 1975, which weighed about 8 pounds and captured images at 0.01 megapixels.
HDR (High Dynamic Range) photography combines multiple exposures of the same scene to capture detail in both shadows and highlights.
Fujifilm's X-series cameras are known for their film simulation modes that replicate the look of classic Fujifilm analog films.
Annie Leibovitz became the last photographer to take a portrait of John Lennon, just hours before his assassination in 1980.
Macro photography captures subjects at life-size or greater magnification, revealing details invisible to the naked eye.
The 'Earthrise' photograph was taken by astronaut William Anders during the Apollo 8 mission on December 24, 1968.
Tilt-shift lenses can control the plane of focus, allowing photographers to keep an entire building sharp or create miniature-effect images.
YouTube was founded in 2005, and its rise created an entirely new profession of video content creators.
Film photography requires chemical development, typically using developer, stop bath, and fixer solutions in a darkroom.
Nikon's F-mount lens system, introduced in 1959, maintained backward compatibility for over 60 years before the Z-mount replaced it.
Astrophotography often requires tracking mounts that compensate for Earth's rotation to capture sharp images of stars and galaxies.
The Polaroid instant camera, introduced in 1948, allowed people to see their photos develop within minutes.
Street photography captures candid moments of everyday life in public places, often emphasizing the human condition.
RED Digital Cinema introduced the RED ONE camera in 2007, bringing 4K resolution to independent filmmakers at a fraction of Hollywood costs.
ISO originally stood for the International Organization for Standardization and measures a camera sensor's sensitivity to light.
Wedding photography is one of the most profitable genres, with top wedding photographers charging $10,000 or more per event.
The 'Raising the Flag on Iwo Jima' photo by Joe Rosenthal was taken on February 23, 1945, and won the Pulitzer Prize.
Computational photography uses software algorithms to enhance images beyond what the lens and sensor alone can capture.
Helmut Newton was known for his provocative and glamorous black-and-white fashion photography for Vogue and other publications.
The 50mm lens is often called the 'nifty fifty' because it approximates the field of view of the human eye and is affordable.
Adobe Lightroom was first released in 2007 and became the standard workflow tool for organizing and editing digital photographs.
FPV (First Person View) drones allow pilots to see through the drone's camera in real-time using goggles, enabling cinematic flight footage.
Landscape photography pioneer Ansel Adams co-founded Group f/64, advocating for sharp-focus, large-format photography.
The Canon EOS 5D Mark II, released in 2008, was the first DSLR to shoot full HD video, transforming independent filmmaking.
Color film became widely available in the 1930s with the introduction of Kodachrome by Kodak in 1935.
A camera's shutter speed controls how long the sensor is exposed to light; faster speeds freeze motion while slower speeds blur it.
Sony's Alpha mirrorless cameras disrupted the market by offering full-frame sensors in smaller, lighter bodies than DSLRs.
The 'Tank Man' photograph from Tiananmen Square on June 5, 1989, is one of the most iconic images of political protest.
Wildlife photography requires patience, long telephoto lenses, and often camouflage to capture animals in their natural habitats.
National Geographic has published photographs since 1905 and is known for setting the standard in nature and cultural photography.
The first color photograph was taken by James Clerk Maxwell in 1861, using three separate exposures through red, green, and blue filters.
Drone photography regulations in the United States require FAA Part 107 certification for commercial drone pilots.
The pancake lens is an extremely thin lens design, typically only 20-40mm thick, prized for portability.
Documentary photography aims to chronicle events and environments truthfully, often to promote social change or historical record.
George Eastman founded Kodak in 1888 with the slogan 'You press the button, we do the rest,' making photography accessible to amateurs.
The mirrorless camera revolution began in earnest in 2018 when Canon, Nikon, and Panasonic all launched full-frame mirrorless systems.
White balance adjusts the color temperature in a photograph to ensure whites appear neutral under different lighting conditions.
The Hasselblad 500C, introduced in 1957, became the go-to medium format camera for commercial and fine art photographers.
Sports photography typically uses fast continuous shooting modes and telephoto lenses of 300mm or longer to capture peak action.
The 'Lunch Atop a Skyscraper' photograph showing 11 men eating lunch on a beam high above New York City was taken on September 20, 1932.
Photo editing on smartphones has become sophisticated, with apps like Snapseed, VSCO, and Lightroom Mobile offering professional-level tools.
The Kodak Brownie camera, introduced in 1900, cost only $1 and brought photography to the mass market for the first time.
Focal length determines a lens's angle of view: wide-angle lenses (under 35mm) capture more, telephoto lenses (over 70mm) magnify distant subjects.
Film grain in analog photography is caused by silver halide crystals; higher ISO films have larger, more visible grain.
The Nikon D1, released in 1999, was one of the first professional digital SLR cameras affordable enough for working photojournalists.
Color grading in video post-production involves adjusting colors to create a specific mood or visual style throughout a film.
Ansel Adams' photograph 'Moonrise, Hernandez, New Mexico' (1941) is one of the most valuable photographs ever sold.
A CMOS sensor converts light into electrical signals and is the most common image sensor type in modern digital cameras.
The first photograph of a human is believed to be in Daguerre's 'Boulevard du Temple' (1838), showing a man getting his shoes shined.
Drone photography has become essential in real estate marketing, providing aerial views of properties and neighborhoods.
Fashion photography emerged as a distinct genre in the early 20th century, closely tied to magazines like Vogue and Harper's Bazaar.
The Canon AE-1, released in 1976, was the first camera with a built-in microprocessor and became one of the best-selling SLRs ever.
Depth of field refers to how much of a scene is in sharp focus, controlled primarily by aperture, focal length, and subject distance.
The daguerreotype process was officially announced to the world on August 19, 1839, now celebrated as World Photography Day.
The Leica M series rangefinder cameras have been favored by street photographers for their compact size and quiet shutter.
Video resolution has progressed from standard definition (480p) to 4K (2160p) and now 8K (4320p) in consumer cameras.
Robert Capa is famous for his D-Day landing photographs and his quote, 'If your pictures aren't good enough, you aren't close enough.'
The histogram is a graphical representation of tonal distribution in an image, helping photographers evaluate exposure accuracy.
Kodak filed for bankruptcy on January 19, 2012, after failing to adapt to the digital photography revolution it helped create.
The GoPro HERO series popularized action cameras, enabling athletes to capture immersive point-of-view footage in extreme sports.
Lightroom's catalog system allows photographers to organize, tag, and search thousands of images efficiently using metadata and keywords.
Infrared photography captures light beyond the visible spectrum, producing surreal images where foliage appears white and skies appear dark.
The Sony A7 (2013) was the first full-frame mirrorless interchangeable-lens camera, setting a new direction for the camera industry.
The golden ratio (approximately 1.618) has been used as a compositional tool in photography and art for centuries.
Photojournalists often work in dangerous conditions, and over 1,400 journalists have been killed worldwide since 1992.
The viewfinder in a DSLR uses a mirror and pentaprism to show the photographer exactly what the lens sees.
Cinematic video often uses a 180-degree shutter rule: the shutter speed should be double the frame rate for natural motion blur.
Edward Weston was a pioneering American photographer known for his sharply focused images of natural forms and landscapes.
The Nikon F, introduced in 1959, established the professional SLR camera system with interchangeable lenses, viewfinders, and motor drives.
Smartphone cameras now use multiple lenses (wide, ultrawide, telephoto) to simulate the versatility of interchangeable lens systems.
Panning is a technique where the camera follows a moving subject with a slow shutter speed, creating a sharp subject against a blurred background.
The Pulitzer Prize for Breaking News Photography replaced the Spot News Photography category in 2000.
DJI released the Phantom 1 drone in 2013, making aerial photography and videography accessible to consumers for the first time.
Negative space in composition refers to the empty area around the subject, which can create powerful, minimalist images.
The first aerial photograph was taken from a hot air balloon by Nadar (Gaspard-Félix Tournachon) over Paris in 1858.
A neutral density filter reduces the amount of light entering the lens, allowing longer exposures in bright conditions.
Peter Lik's 'Phantom' reportedly sold for $6.5 million in 2014, making it one of the most expensive photographs ever sold.
Image stabilization, whether in-body (IBIS) or in-lens (OIS), compensates for camera shake and allows handheld shooting at slower shutter speeds.
The Kodak Instamatic, introduced in 1963, simplified film loading with drop-in cartridges and sold over 50 million units.
RAW files contain unprocessed sensor data, giving photographers maximum flexibility in post-processing compared to JPEG files.
Blue hour, the period of twilight before sunrise or after sunset, produces a deep blue sky ideal for cityscapes and landscapes.
Sebastião Salgado is known for his epic black-and-white documentary projects, including 'Workers' and 'Genesis.'
The first permanent color photograph was taken by Thomas Sutton in 1861, under the direction of physicist James Clerk Maxwell.
Mirrorless cameras eliminated the reflex mirror found in DSLRs, allowing for smaller bodies and electronic viewfinders.
The Nikon D850, released in 2017, combined 45.7 megapixels with fast autofocus, earning widespread critical acclaim as one of the best DSLRs ever made.
A polarizing filter reduces reflections and glare, deepens blue skies, and increases color saturation in landscape photography.
Time-lapse photography captures a series of images at set intervals and plays them back as a video, compressing hours into seconds.
Instagram launched on October 6, 2010, and transformed how people share and consume photographs worldwide.
The Zone System developed by Ansel Adams divided tonal range into 11 zones from pure black (Zone 0) to pure white (Zone X).
Crop factor describes the ratio between a camera sensor's size and a full-frame (35mm) sensor; APS-C sensors typically have a 1.5x crop factor.
Night photography often requires a tripod, wide aperture, and high ISO to capture enough light for a properly exposed image.
The Fujifilm Instax line revived instant photography in the 2010s, proving that physical prints still appeal in the digital age.
Leading lines are compositional elements like roads, fences, or rivers that guide the viewer's eye through the photograph.
The photographic technique of double exposure overlays two images on a single frame, creating surreal and artistic effects.
Canon and Nikon together dominated the professional DSLR market for decades before Sony's mirrorless cameras disrupted the duopoly.
A light meter measures the intensity of light in a scene, helping photographers determine the correct exposure settings.
Vivian Maier was a street photographer whose extraordinary body of work was discovered posthumously at a Chicago auction in 2007.
4K video resolution (3840 × 2160 pixels) has four times the pixel count of 1080p Full HD.
The fisheye lens produces extremely wide-angle images with strong visual distortion, creating a hemispherical or barrel-shaped effect.
World Press Photo of the Year has been awarded since 1955, honoring the single best news photograph annually.
Exposure compensation allows photographers to override the camera's automatic metering to make images brighter or darker.
The Steadicam, invented by Garrett Brown in 1975, revolutionized filmmaking by enabling smooth camera movement while walking or running.
Man Ray was a pioneer of experimental photography, known for techniques like solarization and photograms (which he called 'rayographs').
Chromatic aberration is a lens defect where different wavelengths of light focus at different points, causing color fringing around edges.
The iPhone 4 introduced a front-facing camera in 2010, helping popularize the selfie and changing portrait photography forever.
Medium format cameras use sensors larger than full-frame (35mm), offering higher resolution and better dynamic range for studio and landscape work.
Hyperfocal distance is the focus distance that maximizes depth of field, keeping everything from half that distance to infinity acceptably sharp.
The first photograph taken from space was captured by a V-2 rocket on October 24, 1946.
Drone pilots must maintain visual line of sight with their aircraft under most FAA regulations in the United States.
The Bayer filter array, used in most digital camera sensors, arranges red, green, and blue color filters in a specific pattern with twice as many green pixels.
Cindy Sherman is celebrated for her conceptual self-portraits that challenge ideas about identity, gender, and representation in photography.
A reflector bounces light onto a subject to fill shadows, and collapsible versions are essential tools in portrait photography.
Phase detection autofocus uses pairs of sensors to measure convergence, offering faster focusing than contrast detection in most situations.
The panoramic photograph stitches multiple overlapping images together to create an ultra-wide or 360-degree view of a scene.
Film speed (ISO/ASA) in analog photography determined how sensitive the film emulsion was to light; ISO 400 was a popular all-purpose choice.
Gordon Parks was the first African American photographer and filmmaker to work for Life magazine and the first to direct a major Hollywood film.
Shutter lag is the delay between pressing the shutter button and the camera actually capturing the image; modern cameras have nearly zero lag.
The Large Format camera, using sheet film of 4×5 inches or larger, produces incredibly detailed images favored by fine art and architectural photographers.
Google's Pixel phones pioneered computational photography features like Night Sight and Super Res Zoom using AI and machine learning.
Silhouette photography captures a subject as a dark shape against a bright background, typically during sunrise or sunset.
The Kodak Ektar and Portra film stocks remain popular among analog photographers for their color accuracy and fine grain.
High-speed photography can freeze a bullet in flight or a water droplet mid-splash using shutter speeds of 1/10,000th of a second or faster.
The first known selfie was taken by Robert Cornelius in 1839, a daguerreotype he produced outside his family's lamp store in Philadelphia.
Video frame rates include 24fps (cinematic), 30fps (broadcast), and 60fps (smooth motion); higher rates enable slow-motion playback.
Elliott Erwitt is known for his witty and humorous black-and-white photographs, often featuring dogs and everyday moments.
Back-button focusing separates the autofocus function from the shutter button, giving photographers more control over when the camera focuses.
The Rolleiflex twin-lens reflex camera, introduced in 1929, became an icon of medium format photography used by professionals for decades.
Photo stacking combines multiple images focused at different points to create a single image with extended depth of field, common in macro photography.
The iPhone 7 Plus introduced Portrait Mode with computational bokeh in 2016, simulating the shallow depth of field of professional cameras.
Rembrandt lighting is a portrait lighting technique that creates a small triangle of light on the subject's cheek opposite the light source.
The decisive moment, a concept by Henri Cartier-Bresson, refers to capturing an event at the exact instant that reveals its full significance.
DJI's Mavic series made foldable, portable drones mainstream, allowing photographers to carry aerial cameras in a backpack.
The exposure value (EV) system combines aperture and shutter speed into a single number, simplifying exposure calculations.
Cyanotype is an early photographic printing process that produces blue-toned prints, invented by Sir John Herschel in 1842.
Sensor cleaning is essential for interchangeable-lens cameras, as dust on the sensor creates visible spots in images at small apertures.
Ansel Adams' photograph 'The Tetons and the Snake River' (1942) was included on the Voyager Golden Record sent into space.
A teleconverter is an accessory that attaches between the camera body and lens to increase focal length, typically by 1.4x or 2x.
The Lumière brothers patented the Autochrome process in 1903, creating the first commercially successful color photography method.
Keystoning is the perspective distortion that makes buildings appear to lean backward when photographed with a wide-angle lens tilted upward.
The 85mm focal length is considered ideal for portraiture because it provides a flattering perspective and comfortable working distance.
Stop-motion animation is a filmmaking technique where objects are physically manipulated and photographed frame by frame.
Nick Brandt is known for his powerful black-and-white portraits of African wildlife, shot without telephoto lenses to create intimacy.
A camera obscura is an optical device that projects an inverted image through a small hole, and is the precursor to the modern camera.
The world's most viewed photograph is the default Windows XP wallpaper 'Bliss,' taken by Charles O'Rear in Napa Valley, California.
Lens flare occurs when bright light scatters within a lens, creating artifacts like streaks or circles in the image.
The RED V-RAPTOR camera system can shoot up to 8K resolution at 120 frames per second for high-end cinema production.
Bracketing involves taking multiple shots at different exposures, which can later be combined into an HDR image.
The Olympus OM-1 (1972) was revolutionary for being significantly smaller and lighter than competing professional SLRs.
Light painting is a long-exposure technique where a handheld light source is moved through the frame to create patterns or illuminate subjects.
The first digital camera sold to consumers was the Dycam Model 1 in 1990, offering 376 × 240 pixel resolution.
Photography is the most popular hobby in the world, with over 1.4 trillion photographs taken annually as of the 2020s.
The 'rule of odds' suggests that images with an odd number of subjects are more visually interesting and dynamic than those with even numbers.
Dorothea Lange's work for the Farm Security Administration documented rural poverty during the 1930s and helped shape social documentary photography.
Anamorphic lenses, used in cinema, create a wider field of view and distinctive oval bokeh with horizontal lens flares.
The GoPro HERO3, released in 2012, could shoot 4K video for the first time in a compact, waterproof action camera.
UV filters were originally used to block ultraviolet light on film cameras but are now primarily used to protect the front lens element.
A softbox diffuses and softens artificial light, creating more flattering illumination for portrait and product photography.
The Minolta Maxxum 7000 (1985) was the first SLR with integrated autofocus and motorized film advance, revolutionizing camera design.
Photogrammetry uses overlapping photographs to create 3D models of real-world objects and terrain.
The contact sheet was a standard darkroom tool that printed an entire roll of negatives on a single sheet of photo paper for easy review.
Fujifilm's GFX medium format cameras offer 100+ megapixel sensors in bodies not much larger than full-frame cameras.
Vignetting is the darkening of image corners, which can be an optical defect or an intentional artistic effect.
The Magnum Photos agency was founded in 1947 by Robert Capa, Henri Cartier-Bresson, David Seymour, and George Rodger.
An ND grad (graduated neutral density) filter is darker on top and clear on the bottom, used to balance a bright sky with a darker foreground.
Motion pictures are typically shot at 24 frames per second, a standard established in the late 1920s with the advent of sound film.
Julia Margaret Cameron was a 19th-century photographer known for her soft-focus portraits of Victorian-era celebrities and allegorical subjects.
Focus peaking is an electronic viewfinder feature that highlights in-focus edges with a colored outline, aiding manual focus accuracy.
The Nikon Z9 (2021) was Nikon's flagship mirrorless camera with no mechanical shutter, relying entirely on an electronic shutter.
The sunny 16 rule states that on a sunny day, correct exposure is approximately f/16 with a shutter speed equal to 1/ISO.
Camera traps use motion sensors to automatically photograph wildlife, enabling researchers to study elusive animals without human presence.
The first underwater photograph was taken by William Thompson in 1856 using a camera housed in a waterproof box.
Aspect ratio describes the proportional relationship between an image's width and height; common ratios include 3:2, 4:3, and 16:9.
The Canon EF 70-200mm f/2.8L IS is one of the most popular professional zoom lenses, widely used for sports, weddings, and portraits.
Pixel binning combines data from adjacent pixels to improve low-light performance, a technique widely used in smartphone cameras.
Alfred Stieglitz championed photography as fine art in the early 1900s and founded the influential magazine Camera Work and gallery 291.
The Dutch angle (or German angle) is a camera tilt technique used in filmmaking to create tension, unease, or disorientation.
Lightroom Classic uses a non-destructive editing workflow, meaning the original image file is never permanently altered.
The Sigma Art series lenses gained a reputation for optically rivaling much more expensive manufacturer lenses at lower prices.
The first photograph of Mars was transmitted by Mariner 4 on July 15, 1965.
Chimping is photographer slang for constantly reviewing photos on the camera's LCD screen immediately after shooting.
A monopod provides a single point of stabilization, offering more portability than a tripod while still reducing camera shake.
Yousuf Karsh's portrait of Winston Churchill, taken in 1941, is one of the most reproduced portraits in photographic history.
Dynamic range measures a camera sensor's ability to capture detail in both the brightest and darkest parts of a scene simultaneously.
The world's first photograph, 'View from the Window at Le Gras' by Niépce, required an estimated 8-hour exposure time.
Smartphone computational photography features like Night Mode combine dozens of frames to produce bright, detailed images in near-darkness.
The 35mm film format was adapted from cinema film by Oskar Barnack at Leica in the early 1900s and became the standard for still photography.
Bird's-eye view photography captures subjects from directly above, offering a unique and flattening perspective.
The first photo posted on Instagram was by co-founder Kevin Systrom on July 16, 2010, a picture of a dog near a taco stand.
Buffer depth refers to how many continuous frames a camera can capture before it must slow down to write data to the memory card.
Walker Evans documented American life during the Great Depression for the FSA, influencing generations of documentary photographers.
A ring flash provides even, shadow-free illumination and creates a characteristic circular catchlight in the subject's eyes.
The Hasselblad X1D (2016) was the first compact mirrorless medium format camera, combining portability with exceptional image quality.
Hyperlapse is an advanced time-lapse technique where the camera moves a significant distance between each frame, creating a flowing motion effect.
The 'Napalm Girl' photograph by Nick Ut, taken during the Vietnam War on June 8, 1972, won the Pulitzer Prize and influenced public opinion about the war.
Lens compression is a visual effect of telephoto lenses that makes distant objects appear closer together and larger relative to foreground elements.
The ARRI ALEXA cinema camera system became the industry standard for Hollywood productions, used in most Academy Award-nominated films.
A circular polarizer rotates to control the amount of polarization, allowing photographers to fine-tune reflections and sky contrast.
The pinhole camera is the simplest camera design, using just a tiny hole to project an inverted image onto film or a sensor.
Eye autofocus (Eye AF) was pioneered by Sony and can detect and focus on a subject's eye, revolutionizing portrait photography.
Weegee (Arthur Fellig) was a press photographer famous for his stark black-and-white images of crime scenes and urban life in 1930s-1950s New York City.
The DJI Mini series drones weigh under 250 grams, exempting them from registration requirements in many countries.
Diffraction occurs at very small apertures (f/16 and beyond), slightly softening images despite the increased depth of field.
Photoshop's Content-Aware Fill, introduced in 2010, uses AI to intelligently remove objects from images by filling in the background.
The Ilford HP5 Plus is one of the most popular black-and-white films, known for its versatility and pushability to higher ISOs.
Camera RAW files from different manufacturers have different extensions: .CR2/.CR3 (Canon), .NEF (Nikon), .ARW (Sony), .RAF (Fujifilm).
Berenice Abbott documented the transformation of New York City in the 1930s in her project 'Changing New York.'
The first digital backs for medium format cameras appeared in the 1990s, costing as much as a luxury car.
Kelvin is the unit used to measure color temperature; daylight is approximately 5500K, tungsten lighting is about 3200K.
The concept of 'chimney lighting' uses a narrow strip of light to dramatically illuminate a portion of the subject's face.
An intervalometer is a device that triggers the camera shutter at preset intervals, essential for time-lapse photography.
The Sony A1 (2021) shoots 50 megapixel stills at 30 frames per second with full autofocus tracking, pushing the limits of camera technology.
Platinum prints, an alternative photographic process, produce images with subtle tonal gradation and exceptional archival longevity.
The crop sensor introduces a crop factor that effectively multiplies the focal length of any lens mounted on the camera body.
Diane Arbus was known for her photographs of marginalized communities, challenging conventional notions of beauty and normalcy in the 1960s.
Log video profiles capture a flat, desaturated image that preserves maximum dynamic range for later color grading in post-production.
The first camera phone was the Sharp J-SH04, released in Japan in November 2000.
Split toning applies different colors to the highlights and shadows of a photograph, creating distinctive stylized looks.
Bird photography requires long telephoto lenses (400mm+) and fast autofocus to capture small, fast-moving subjects in flight.
The Rollei 35 (1966) was the smallest 35mm camera when introduced and remained popular as a compact travel camera for years.
ETTR (Expose to the Right) is a technique of deliberately overexposing to maximize data in the highlights, then correcting in post-processing.
Adobe's Camera Raw plugin and Lightroom share the same processing engine for developing raw files.
The wet plate collodion process, popular in the 1850s-1880s, required photographers to coat, expose, and develop glass plates while still wet.
Alexander Rodchenko pioneered constructivist photography in the 1920s with dramatic diagonal compositions and unusual viewpoints.
Film cameras experienced a surprising resurgence in the 2020s, with prices for popular models like the Contax T2 skyrocketing.
A snoot is a tube-shaped light modifier that narrows the beam of a flash or strobe for precise, focused illumination.
The Nikon Z8 (2023) packed Z9-level performance into a smaller, lighter body, making flagship features more accessible.
The 24-70mm f/2.8 zoom lens is considered the 'holy trinity' workhorse, covering a versatile range from wide-angle to short telephoto.
National Geographic photographer Joel Sartore's 'Photo Ark' project aims to document every species in captivity before they disappear.
Pixel peeping is the practice of examining images at 100% crop to evaluate sharpness, a practice that can lead to overemphasizing technical perfection.
The first iPhone with optical image stabilization was the iPhone 6 Plus, released in 2014.
B-roll is supplementary footage used in video production to provide visual context, cover transitions, or add variety to the main narrative.
Josef Sudek, known as the 'Poet of Prague,' created atmospheric still-life and landscape photographs using natural window light.
Moire patterns appear in digital images when fine, repetitive details in a subject conflict with the pixel grid of the camera sensor.
The Tamron 28-75mm f/2.8 Di III became a popular affordable alternative to manufacturer's first-party zoom lenses for mirrorless cameras.
The World Photography Day is celebrated annually on August 19, commemorating the announcement of the daguerreotype process in 1839.
The bellows system on large format cameras allows extreme movements for perspective control and selective focus.
Photo essays use a series of photographs to tell a story or explore a theme, combining narrative and visual elements.
The Brenizer method (bokeh panorama) combines multiple telephoto shots into a panorama, creating an image with extremely shallow depth of field.
Ken Burns popularized the technique of slowly panning and zooming across still photographs in documentary films, now called the 'Ken Burns effect.'
Cross-processing is a technique of deliberately developing film in chemicals intended for a different film type, producing unusual color shifts.
Canon's Dual Pixel CMOS AF technology uses each pixel on the sensor for both imaging and phase-detection autofocus.
André Kertész was a Hungarian photographer whose innovative compositions and use of light influenced many 20th-century photographers.
The difference between optical zoom and digital zoom is that optical zoom uses lens mechanics while digital zoom simply crops and enlarges the image.
Focus stacking in landscape photography combines images focused at different distances to achieve front-to-back sharpness.
The carte de visite, a small albumin print mounted on card stock, was the first widely traded format for portrait photographs in the 1860s.
Blackmagic Design's Pocket Cinema Camera brought cinema-quality video recording at a fraction of professional cinema camera prices.
The Fujifilm X100 series compact cameras gained a cult following for their retro design, fixed 23mm lens, and hybrid viewfinder.
A beauty dish is a parabolic light modifier that produces a contrasty, wrapped light popular in fashion and beauty photography.
The oldest surviving photograph from nature is Niépce's 'View from the Window at Le Gras,' created around 1826 or 1827.
Weston's 1930 photograph of a green pepper, 'Pepper No. 30,' is considered one of the greatest still-life photographs ever taken.
ProRes and DNxHR are professional video codecs designed for editing, offering high quality with manageable file sizes.
The earliest panoramic cameras rotated during exposure to capture extra-wide images onto a curved film plane.
Daido Moriyama is a Japanese photographer known for his high-contrast, blurry, grainy black-and-white street photography.
Memory cards for cameras have evolved from CompactFlash to SD to CFexpress, with each generation offering faster write speeds and larger capacities.
Remapping or lens profiling corrects distortion, vignetting, and chromatic aberration specific to each lens model in post-processing software.
The first photo transmitted by wire was sent from London to New York in 1935, taking about 7 minutes.
Drone photography's bird's-eye perspective has become iconic in real estate, travel, wedding, and landscape photography.
The Pentax K1000 (1976) was a beloved manual-focus film SLR used by photography students for decades due to its simplicity and reliability.
Color space defines the range of colors a camera can capture or a display can show; sRGB is standard for web, Adobe RGB for print.
Mary Ellen Mark was a celebrated documentary photographer known for her compassionate, unflinching images of marginalized communities.
The mechanical shutter in a camera uses physical curtains that open and close to control exposure; electronic shutters do this digitally.
A mattebox attaches to the front of a cinema lens to block stray light and hold filters, giving video rigs a professional look.
Eadweard Muybridge's sequential photographs of a galloping horse in 1878 proved that all four hooves leave the ground simultaneously.
Pixel shift resolution technology moves the sensor in one-pixel increments to capture multiple shots that combine into a higher-resolution image.
The Zone System by Ansel Adams requires visualizing the final print before the exposure is made, a concept called 'previsualization.'
GoPro's SuperView mode uses a wider field of view than the standard setting, immersing viewers in the action.
Sabattier effect (often called solarization) is a partial or complete reversal of tones in a photograph caused by re-exposure during development.
The Canon RF mount, introduced in 2018 alongside the EOS R, offers a shorter flange distance than the EF mount for improved lens design.
Autochrome plates used dyed grains of potato starch to create color images and were the dominant color process from 1907 to the 1930s.
Edward Curtis spent over 20 years photographing Native American peoples, producing the monumental 20-volume work 'The North American Indian.'
Barrel distortion causes straight lines to curve outward, common in wide-angle lenses; pincushion distortion curves them inward, common in telephotos.
The Leica Q series fixed-lens full-frame cameras are prized for their combination of image quality, build, and compact design.
The GoPro MAX is a 360-degree camera that captures spherical video, allowing creators to choose their framing angle in post-production.
The first X-ray photograph was taken by Wilhelm Röntgen in 1895, showing his wife's hand complete with her wedding ring.
Flat lay photography arranges objects on a flat surface and photographs them from directly above, popular for food and product content.
Photographic memory cards can fail without warning; the 3-2-1 backup rule (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite) protects against data loss.
Fan Ho was a Chinese photographer and filmmaker known for his masterful use of light and shadow in mid-20th-century Hong Kong street scenes.
The equivalent focal length on a crop sensor camera is calculated by multiplying the lens focal length by the crop factor.
The first photograph of a solar eclipse was taken by Johann Julius Friedrich Berkowski on July 28, 1851.
Lightroom presets are saved combinations of editing settings that can be applied to photos for consistent style and faster workflow.
Pixel density, measured in pixels per inch (PPI), determines print sharpness; 300 PPI is the standard for high-quality photo prints.
The Contax G2 (1996) was an autofocus rangefinder camera with titanium construction and Carl Zeiss lenses, now a collector's item.
Deadpan photography uses a neutral, detached perspective to document subjects without emotional bias, as seen in the work of the Düsseldorf School.
Video color spaces like Rec. 709 (HD) and Rec. 2020 (UHD/HDR) define how colors are encoded and displayed on screens.
The first color underwater photograph was taken by Charles Martin and W.H. Longley for National Geographic in 1926.
A gobo (go between) is placed between a light source and the subject or background to create patterned shadows like window frames or foliage.
The Pentax 67 medium format SLR, nicknamed 'the beast,' was beloved for its sharp Takumar lenses and 6×7cm negatives.
Philip-Lorca diCorcia combined candid street photography with carefully set-up lighting, blurring the line between documentary and staged imagery.
Camera shake at slow shutter speeds can be estimated by the reciprocal rule: minimum safe handheld speed equals 1/(focal length).
The DNG (Digital Negative) format was created by Adobe as a universal raw file format to prevent proprietary format obsolescence.
Ilse Bing, known as the 'Queen of the Leica,' was a pioneering photojournalist and artist in 1930s Paris.
A speed booster (focal reducer) adapter increases the effective aperture and field of view when adapting larger format lenses to smaller sensor cameras.
The Canon EOS R5 (2020) was the first hybrid camera to offer 8K raw video recording internally.
Burnt highlights (clipped whites) and crushed blacks (clipped shadows) represent lost data that cannot be recovered in post-processing.
The view camera movements of tilt, shift, swing, rise, and fall allow precise control of focus plane and perspective.
Andreas Gursky's 'Rhein II' sold for $4.3 million in 2011, setting a record at the time for the most expensive photograph sold at auction.
A gray card reflects exactly 18% of light and is used to set accurate exposure and white balance.
The Global Shutter reads all pixels simultaneously, eliminating the rolling shutter distortion (wobble) common in fast-moving video subjects.
Tintype photography, popular during the American Civil War, produced images on thin metal plates coated with light-sensitive chemicals.
Sony's full-frame mirrorless cameras accounted for approximately 50% of the full-frame market by the early 2020s.
The Voigtländer Bessa series rangefinder cameras offer affordable manual-focus alternatives compatible with Leica M-mount lenses.
William Eggleston is widely credited with legitimizing color photography as a fine art medium with his 1976 solo exhibition at MoMA.
The Zeiss Otus lens series is regarded as one of the sharpest lens lines ever made, setting optical benchmarks for full-frame photography.
A cinemagraph is a still photograph in which a minor, repeated movement occurs, creating a mesmerizing looping animation.
Multi-exposure noise reduction stacks several identical frames and averages them, reducing random noise while preserving detail.
The Rolleicord was Rollei's affordable twin-lens reflex camera, making medium format photography accessible to amateurs.
Drone no-fly zones include airports, military bases, national parks (in the US), and other sensitive areas established by aviation authorities.
Contrast detection autofocus works by analyzing image sharpness and is typically slower but more accurate than phase detection in some situations.
Imogen Cunningham was a renowned American photographer known for her botanical photography, nudes, and industrial landscapes.
The first daguerreotype portrait in America was taken by Robert Cornelius in October 1839.
A graduated filter transitions from colored or neutral on one half to clear on the other, useful for adding warmth or managing sky exposure.
Video LUTs (Look-Up Tables) are preset color transformations that quickly apply a specific color grade to footage.
The Bronica SQ-Ai was a modular medium format SLR system popular for studio and landscape work in the 1990s.
Dodging and burning are printing techniques from the darkroom era that lighten (dodge) or darken (burn) specific areas of a print.
The Panasonic Lumix GH5 is known for its video capabilities, including 4K 60fps recording, making it popular among videographers and YouTubers.
Lenticular printing creates images that change or show depth as the viewing angle shifts, using an array of tiny lenses.
Thread mounts like the M42 screw mount were universal lens standards before bayonet mounts became dominant in the 1970s.
Henri Cartier-Bresson almost exclusively used a 50mm lens on his Leica, believing it best matched the human eye's perspective.
The Olympus Tough series cameras are waterproof, shockproof, and freeze-proof, designed for extreme outdoor photography.
The daguerreotype required subjects to remain still for several minutes due to long exposure times, which is why nobody smiles in early photographs.
The selfie stick was invented long before smartphones; a Japanese photographer patented an 'extender stick' for cameras in 1983.
Flash sync speed is the fastest shutter speed at which the camera can synchronize with a flash; typically 1/200s or 1/250s.
The DJI Air 2S features a 1-inch sensor, offering significantly better image quality than smaller-sensor drones.
Galen Rowell was a renowned landscape and adventure photographer known for his work in the Sierra Nevada and Himalayas.
HEIF (High Efficiency Image Format) captures more color data than JPEG at similar file sizes and is used by modern smartphones.
The Ansco company, a major American film and camera manufacturer, competed with Kodak for decades before ceasing operations in the 1980s.
Photographic film is still manufactured by companies including Kodak, Ilford, and Fujifilm, serving a dedicated community of analog enthusiasts.
A tripod's load capacity should exceed the weight of the camera and lens combined to ensure stability and prevent tipping.
The National Press Photographers Association (NPPA) has promoted photojournalism ethics and excellence since 1946.
High-key photography uses predominantly bright tones and minimal shadows to create a light, airy, and optimistic mood.
The DJI Inspire series drones feature dual-operator control, with one person flying and another controlling the camera independently.
Adams, Cunningham, and Weston were members of Group f/64, advocating for sharply focused photographs that celebrated photography's unique qualities.
Photoshop layers allow editors to stack multiple image elements and adjustments non-destructively, building complex composites.
The Sony A7S series prioritizes low-light video performance, with the A7S III capable of shooting clean footage at extremely high ISOs.
Shutter count indicates the number of actuations a camera's mechanical shutter has performed and is used to estimate its remaining lifespan.
The ambrotype was a photographic process popular in the 1850s, producing a positive image on glass using the wet collodion process.
James Nachtwey is one of the most acclaimed war photographers, known for his unflinching documentation of conflict and social issues.
The 'Sunny 16' rule was a standard estimation method for exposure before cameras had built-in light meters.
Photo booths were first patented on September 15, 1888, and modern versions remain popular at events and weddings.
The Hasselblad camera used on the Moon during the Apollo 11 mission in 1969 was left on the lunar surface to save weight for rock samples.
Bracketed HDR photography became automated in the 2010s, with cameras firing three or more exposures with a single press of the shutter.
Sally Mann is known for her intimate and controversial black-and-white photographs of her children, landscapes, and decomposition.
The sensor size hierarchy from smallest to largest goes: 1-inch, Micro Four Thirds, APS-C, Full Frame, Medium Format.
The Profoto B1 was the first battery-powered studio strobe with TTL metering, freeing portrait photographers from power outlet locations.
Wet plate collodion photography has experienced a modern revival, with workshops and new practitioners embracing the 19th-century process.
The first Pulitzer Prize for Photography was awarded in 1942 to Milton Brooks for his photo of striking auto workers.
Giclée printing uses high-quality inkjet technology to produce fine art photographic prints with exceptional color accuracy and longevity.
The Canon 1D X series flagship cameras are built for sports and press photography, offering extreme durability and fast shooting speeds.
Photographic filters in the darkroom, like multigrade filters, controlled the contrast of black-and-white prints on variable-contrast paper.
The Insta360 cameras popularized 360-degree consumer video, enabling creators to capture immersive content and reframe shots in post.
Shooting in manual mode gives the photographer full control over aperture, shutter speed, and ISO, eliminating camera guesswork.
The stereoscope, popular in the Victorian era, used paired photographs to create a 3D viewing experience.
Photographic paper comes in multiple surfaces including glossy, matte, lustre, and metallic, each producing a different look and feel.
The concept of megapixels refers to millions of pixels; a 24-megapixel sensor captures images at 6000 × 4000 pixels.
The 'V for Victory' photograph of Churchill was captured when Karsh snatched Churchill's cigar away moments before clicking the shutter.
The term 'paparazzi' comes from the character Paparazzo, a photographer in Federico Fellini's 1960 film 'La Dolce Vita.'
DJI's ActiveTrack feature allows drones to automatically follow and film a moving subject using computer vision.
The world's largest camera was built by George R. Lawrence in 1900 and weighed 1,400 pounds, producing mammoth prints for a railroad company.
Topaz Labs and DxO are known for AI-powered photo enhancement software that can denoise, sharpen, and upscale images beyond camera limitations.
